skill.name: implement-design
skill.description: Translates Figma designs into production-ready code through a structured Figma MCP workflow for context, screenshots, assets, and visual validation.
skill.summary: Implement Figma designs as production UI with Figma MCP context and visual checks.
skill.author: figma

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell execution, hardcoded URLs, and weak cryptography patterns, but review found a Markdown workflow with no executable code. The shell and cryptography hits are false positives from inline code formatting and design-related text. The remaining low risk is normal Figma MCP and documentation URL usage.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution, but the reviewed lines are Markdown inline code and fenced examples for Figma MCP tool names, placeholder arguments, and design tokens. No executable shell script, Ruby code, or command substitution is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and the cited text is rendered documentation for MCP calls. I found no shell, Ruby, or script file that would execute these backtick-delimited strings.

low_findings.title: Figma URLs Are Documentation and Input Examples
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings point to a required user-provided Figma URL format, sample Figma links, and official Figma documentation links. These links document expected inputs and resources; they do not send local data or credentials to an external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible examples and reference links in the skill instructions. There is no code path that performs unauthorized network requests or exfiltrates secrets.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Is a Textual False Positive
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithms across many lines, but review found no cryptographic API use. The hits align with ordinary design workflow text such as the skill name, design descriptions, and design system guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No encryption, hashing, cipher, or password-handling code appears in SKILL.md. The static hits are caused by natural language in a design implementation document.

low_findings.title: Legitimate Figma MCP Network Dependency
low_findings.description: The skill requires a Figma MCP server and may use Figma file URLs or localhost asset URLs supplied by that server. This is expected for the advertised function, but users should only use trusted Figma files and configured MCP servers.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dependency on Figma MCP and localhost assets is explicitly documented and necessary for the skill. I found no evidence that the skill initiates network access beyond user-directed Figma workflow data.

content.user_title: Implement Figma Designs in Production Code
content.value_statement: Teams lose time translating visual designs into consistent frontend code. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through Figma MCP context, screenshots, assets, and validation steps.
actual_capabilities: Extracts file keys and node IDs from Figma design URLs.
Uses Figma MCP tools to fetch design context, metadata, and screenshots.
Guides asset handling for images, icons, and SVGs served by the MCP server.
Maps Figma layout, typography, color, and component data to project conventions.
Promotes reuse of existing components and design system tokens.
Defines visual validation checks for layout, typography, colors, states, responsiveness, and assets.
limitations: Requires a configured Figma MCP server or Figma desktop MCP connection.
Cannot access private Figma files without the user’s normal permissions and setup.
Does not provide executable implementation scripts by itself.
Complex designs may require metadata-based node selection before implementation.

use_cases.title: Build a New Component
use_cases.description: Convert a selected Figma button, card, or form control into a project-ready component that follows local conventions.

use_cases.title: Review Design Fidelity
use_cases.description: Compare an implemented interface against a Figma screenshot and identify spacing, typography, asset, and responsive mismatches.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Workflow
use_cases.description: Give contributors a consistent process for fetching Figma context, mapping tokens, reusing components, and documenting deviations.

prompt_templates.title: Implement a Simple Component
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner task with one Figma node.
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement this Figma component using the project’s existing component patterns: [paste Figma URL]. Fetch the design context and screenshot first.

prompt_templates.title: Match an Existing Design System
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate task that must reuse local UI primitives.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build this Figma design with our existing design system components where possible: [paste Figma URL]. Map Figma tokens to project tokens and note any deviations.

prompt_templates.title: Implement a Full Screen
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced layout with multiple child sections.
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement this full Figma screen: [paste Figma URL]. If the context is too large, fetch metadata, select major child nodes, and implement each section incrementally.

prompt_templates.title: Validate and Refine Fidelity
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of an existing implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the current implementation to this Figma reference: [paste Figma URL]. Use the screenshot as the source of truth and fix visual differences in layout, typography, color, assets, and states.

output_examples.input: A user asks to implement a Figma button component from a shared design URL.
output_examples.output: A reusable button component that follows project naming, uses design tokens, includes the required variants, and matches the Figma screenshot.

output_examples.input: A dashboard frame is too large for one design context response.
output: A metadata-based implementation plan for header, sidebar, content, and card sections.
A staged build that fetches only the needed child nodes.
A final visual check against the Figma screenshot.

output_examples.input: An existing page looks close but does not match the Figma reference.
output_examples.output: A focused set of fixes for spacing, typography, colors, responsive behavior, and asset handling, with documented reasons for any intentional differences.

best_practices: Fetch design context and a screenshot before writing implementation code.
Reuse project components and design tokens before creating new UI primitives.
Validate against the Figma screenshot throughout implementation, not only at the end.
anti_patterns: Implementing from memory or screenshots without fetching structured Figma context.
Adding new icon packages when Figma MCP already provides the required assets.
Copying Figma output directly without adapting it to project conventions.

faq.question: Does this skill require Figma MCP?
faq.answer: Yes. It depends on Figma MCP or Figma desktop MCP to fetch design context, screenshots, metadata, and assets.

faq.question: Can it work from a selected Figma desktop node?
faq.answer: Yes. With figma-desktop MCP, the selected node can be used without a Figma URL.

faq.question: Does it generate final code automatically?
faq.answer: It provides the workflow for implementation. The assistant still writes code using the project’s framework and conventions.

faq.question: How does it handle large Figma frames?
faq.answer: It recommends fetching metadata first, identifying important child nodes, and then fetching each node separately.

faq.question: Can it use existing design system components?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow prioritizes existing components, tokens, naming conventions, and project data patterns.

faq.question: What should users verify before finishing?
faq.answer: Users should check layout, typography, colors, interactive states, responsive behavior, assets, and accessibility against the Figma screenshot.
